element.short: a kind of atom, set by the number of protons in its nucleus (Z).
element.long: An element is a chemical species defined by its atomic number Z. The atlas catalogs five — hydrogen, helium, neon, sodium, mercury — chosen for the variety of spectra they produce.

ionization-energy.short: the energy needed to remove one electron from a neutral atom.
ionization-energy.long: Measured in eV. Hydrogen: 13.598 eV. Mercury: 10.437 eV. The Lyman series of hydrogen converges at this threshold.

spectral-line.short: a single emitted or absorbed wavelength tied to one atomic transition.
spectral-line.long: A spectral line records the photon energy of an upper-to-lower level jump. The atlas stores vacuum wavelengths in nanometres, sourced from NIST ASD.

wavelength.short: the spatial period of a light wave, usually in nanometres.
wavelength.long: Related to frequency by λν = c and to photon energy by E = hc/λ. Visible light spans roughly 380 to 750 nm.

series.short: a family of spectral lines sharing the same lower energy level.
series.long: Hydrogen has Lyman (n_low=1), Balmer (2), Paschen (3), Brackett (4), Pfund (5). Lines within a series bunch toward a limit at the ionization edge.

rydberg-formula.long: Predicts each hydrogen spectral line from two integers. R_H ≈ 1.097 × 10⁷ m⁻¹ includes the reduced-mass correction for hydrogen.

energy-level.short: one of the discrete energies a bound electron can have.
energy-level.long: Hydrogen's bound levels follow E_n = -13.598 eV / n². Multi-electron levels are labeled by term symbols and are coupled by spin-orbit and electron-electron interactions.

ground-state.short: the lowest energy level an atom can occupy.
ground-state.long: Hydrogen's ground state is 1s, ²S_{1/2}, at -13.598 eV. Atoms rest here unless excited by collisions, photons, or fields.

term-symbol.short: the label ²S+¹L_J that names a coupled atomic energy level.
term-symbol.long: Encodes total spin (2S+1), total orbital angular momentum (L = S, P, D, F, …), and their coupled total J. Sodium's D₂ upper level is 3²P_{3/2}.

fine-structure.short: the small splitting of levels by spin-orbit coupling.
fine-structure.long: Splits the sodium 3p level into ²P_{1/2} and ²P_{3/2}, producing the D₁/D₂ doublet 0.6 nm apart. Scales with α² for hydrogen-like ions.

transition-type.short: the multipole channel that drives a radiative jump.
transition-type.long: E1 (electric dipole), M1 (magnetic dipole), E2 (electric quadrupole), or forbidden. E1 is fastest; the others are suppressed by small powers of αZ or ka₀.

forbidden-transition.short: a jump that violates the leading-order multipole selection rules.
forbidden-transition.long: Strictly forbidden in pure LS coupling but often weakly allowed via spin-orbit mixing. Mercury's 253.65 nm 6³P₁→6¹S₀ is the canonical example.

parity.short: whether a state is even or odd under inversion through the origin.
parity.long: E1 transitions flip parity; M1 and E2 preserve it. Parity is the most-cited single selection rule.

constant.short: a physical quantity that does not vary with the system.
constant.long: Some constants are fixed exactly by the SI (c, h, e, k_B, N_A). Others, like α and m_e, are measured and carry CODATA uncertainties.

si-base-unit.short: one of the seven units of the International System.
si-base-unit.long: Metre, second, kelvin, mole, kilogram, ampere, candela. Since 2019 they are defined by fixing seven constants exactly.

fine-structure-constant.long: Sets the strength of the electromagnetic interaction at low energy. CODATA 2022: α = 7.2973525643(11) × 10⁻³.

bohr-radius.short: the natural length scale of atomic physics, a₀ ≈ 0.529 Å.
bohr-radius.long: Defined by a₀ = ℏ/(m_e c α). The most-probable radius of hydrogen's 1s state in the infinite-nucleus-mass limit.

unit.short: a named quantity used to express a measured value.
unit.long: Different communities prefer different units for the same dimension — energy may be J, eV, cm⁻¹, Hz, or E_h depending on context.

electronvolt.short: the energy one electron gains crossing 1 V.
electronvolt.long: 1 eV = 1.602 176 634 × 10⁻¹⁹ J, exact since the 2019 SI redefinition fixed e. Convenient unit for atomic and molecular energies.

wavenumber.short: energy expressed as reciprocal length, usually cm⁻¹.
wavenumber.long: Defined by ν̃ = E/(hc). 1 eV ≈ 8065.544 cm⁻¹. The spectroscopist's canonical energy proxy.

identity.short: a closed-form equation tying physical quantities together.
identity.long: The atlas catalogues landmark identities: E = hν, E = hc/λ, λν = c, the Rydberg energy, the Bohr radius, and α's definition.

planck-einstein.short: E = hν — a photon's energy is h times its frequency.
planck-einstein.long: Equivalently E = hc/λ. With h and c exact since 2019, the constant hc = 1239.842 eV·nm is exact to all decimals shown.

special-function.short: a named solution to a recurring differential equation.
special-function.long: Legendre, Laguerre, Hermite, Bessel, hypergeometric, and others. They form orthogonal bases for separable PDE solutions.

orthogonality.short: two functions integrate to zero against a chosen weight.
orthogonality.long: For Legendre polynomials on [-1, 1]: ∫ P_m P_n dx = 2/(2n+1) δ_{mn}. Orthogonality lets a function be decomposed into a unique sum of basis functions.

spherical-harmonic.short: the orthogonal basis on the sphere indexed by l and m.
spherical-harmonic.long: Y_l^m(θ, φ) = √[(2l+1)/(4π) · (l-m)!/(l+m)!] P_l^m(cosθ) e^{imφ}. They give the angular part of hydrogen's wavefunction.

coord-system.short: a labeling of space that lets a PDE separate into pieces.
coord-system.long: Eleven systems R-separate the 3D Helmholtz equation. The hydrogen Stark effect, for instance, separates in parabolic coordinates, not in spherical.

polytope.short: the higher-dimensional cousin of a polygon or polyhedron.
polytope.long: Regular polytopes have congruent regular faces and identical vertex figures. They exist in every dimension; 4D has six, every dimension ≥ 5 has three.

platonic-solid.short: one of the five regular convex polyhedra in 3D.
platonic-solid.long: Tetrahedron {3,3}, cube {4,3}, octahedron {3,4}, dodecahedron {5,3}, icosahedron {3,5}. Paired by duality: cube↔octahedron, dodecahedron↔icosahedron, tetrahedron self-dual.

lattice.short: a discrete set of points repeating by translation.
lattice.long: Specified by basis vectors. 2D has 5 Bravais classes; 3D has 14. The conventional cell records its axis lengths and angles.
lattice.source: International Tables for Crystallography Vol. A

bravais-lattice.short: one of the 14 unique 3D translational lattices.
bravais-lattice.long: Classified by crystal system (cubic, tetragonal, orthorhombic, hexagonal, rhombohedral, monoclinic, triclinic) and centering (P, I, F, C).

tiling.short: a covering of a space by repeating shapes without gaps.
tiling.long: Regular tilings use one regular polygon with the same arrangement at every vertex. The Euclidean plane admits exactly three; the hyperbolic plane admits infinitely many.

curved-space.short: a Riemannian manifold with nonzero curvature.
curved-space.long: The atlas restricts attention to constant-curvature model spaces: Euclidean (K=0), spherical (K=+1), and hyperbolic (K=-1).

symmetry-group.short: the set of transformations that leave a structure unchanged.
symmetry-group.long: Discrete point groups label molecular symmetry; continuous Lie groups label gauge and rotational symmetries; space groups combine point operations with translations.

lie-group.short: a continuous symmetry group with a smooth manifold structure.
lie-group.long: The classical series A_n = su(n+1), B_n = so(2n+1), C_n = sp(2n), D_n = so(2n) cover most physical symmetries; G₂, F₄, E₆, E₇, E₈ are exceptional.
